did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a multi-class Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and six binary CNNs with EfficientNetB5 as the backbone, to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, and benign keratosis) for computer-aided diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a new computer-aided diagnosis framework using deep neural networks (EfficientNetB5) and game theory. It describes specific methods (multi-class and binary CNNs, confidence evaluation), reports results from experiments on the ISIC dataset (AUROC, BACC metrics), and includes an ablation study and comparison with prior works, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Computer Aided Diagnosis of Melanoma Using Deep Neural Networks and Game Theory: Application on Dermoscopic Images of Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study presents a computer-aided diagnosis framework for melanoma using deep neural networks and game theory to enhance both performance and decision transparency. The approach employs a multi-class Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and six binary CNNs, all utilizing EfficientNetB5 as the backbone, to classify dermoscopic images of melanoma, nevus, and benign keratosis. The system evaluates prediction confidence to cluster lesions into confident, medium, and uncertain levels, refining diagnoses for difficult cases through a hierarchical strategy. The model was evaluated on the public ISIC 2018 dataset comprising 8,917 lesions. The proposed framework achieved an average AUROC of 0.96 and improved the balanced accuracy (BACC) to 86%, outperforming existing methods. These results suggest the system is effective for assisting dermatologists, particularly in clinical settings with atypical or difficult-to-diagnose pigmented lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of the computer-aided diagnosis framework; 1,113 lesions in the ISIC 2018 subset used for experiments.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, Benign Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Nevus, and Benign Keratosis. It also employs six binary classification tasks (one-versus-all and one-versus-one strategies) to distinguish between these classes. The model outputs predicted probabilities for class membership, which are used to determine the final discrete diagnosis.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study used a subset of this dataset focusing on three specific classes: Melanoma, Nevi, and Benign Keratosis. The original dataset contained 10,017 images annotated into seven classes, but only 8,917 lesions were used for the experiments.

use.training: Used for training the multi-class CNN and six binary CNNs. The training set was augmented with generated data (horizontal flip, vertical flip, rotation, width and height shift) to balance class distribution, resulting in a final training set of 9,000 images (2,000 per class).
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning (grid-search for confidence thresholds u1 and u2) and model selection.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the final performance of the framework, reporting BACC and AUROC metrics.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating curve

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To aggregate results and generate ROC curves, ensuring robustness of performance metrics.
To determine optimal hyper-parameters (u1 and u2) for confidence clustering by maximizing balanced accuracy on the validation set.
To report the central tendency and variability of performance metrics (BACC, AUROC) across the five experimental runs.
To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the models across different classification thresholds.
To evaluate classification performance while accounting for class imbalance in the dataset.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the ability of the classifier to distinguish between classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (mean AUROC, BACC, and standard deviation) across five experimental runs, but no statistical significance testing (e.g., t-test, ANOVA) was employed to validate the superiority of the proposed framework over prior works.

ISIC 2018 (Multi-class Classification).notes: The proposed framework outperformed all compared methods. It improved the balanced accuracy (BACC) from 77% (best prior method) to 86%. It achieved an AUROC improvement of 5% for Melanoma, 4% for Benign Keratosis, and 8% for Nevi compared to prior works. Results were aggregated from five experimental runs.

ISIC 2018 (Individual Model Performance).notes: The BEK-vs-ALL classifier achieved the best performance among one-versus-all classifiers (BACC 90%). The NEV-vs-BEK classifier performed best among one-versus-one classifiers (BACC 94%). The most difficult distinction was Nevi from Melanoma (BACC 87%). The 3-class model reached a BACC of 84%, which was a 2% improvement over the entire pipeline's base 3-class model performance before confidence clustering.

Validation Set (Ablation Study for Hyper.notes: The best combination of hyper-parameters u1 and u2 was found to be 0.1 and 0.5 respectively, maximizing the detection of Melanoma. Other combinations like (0.3, 0.5) yielded a MEL-AUROC of 0.95.

architecture_details.modification: Original classification layer replaced with a new fully connected (FC) layer
architecture_details.ensemble_structure: Hierarchical architecture combining seven CNNs: one multi-class CNN (s3) and six binary CNNs (three one-versus-all (ova) and three one-versus-one (ovo))

training_details.data_augmentation: Horizontal flip, vertical flip, rotation, width shift, height shift

classification_or_output_head.nodes: 2 for binary classification, 3 for ternary classification

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 456 × 456 using bicubic interpolation
Color standardization using the gray world color constancy algorithm to deal with color variability
Data augmentation (horizontal flip, vertical flip, rotation, width shift, height shift) to reduce class imbalance effects

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal flip, vertical flip, rotation, width shift, and height shift were applied to the training set to reduce class imbalance effects. Specifically, generated data from the training set was used to balance the distribution, resulting in a final training set of 2,000 images per class for Melanoma and Benign Keratosis, and 5,000 for Nevi.

conclusions: The proposed framework, combining a multi-class CNN and six binary CNNs with EfficientNetB5 as the backbone, achieved an average AUROC of 0.96 and a balanced accuracy (BACC) of 86% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming existing methods.
The system improved the BACC of the best compared method from 77% to 86%, representing a 9% improvement.
The framework demonstrated high performance for specific lesion classes, with AUROC scores of 0.93 for melanoma, 0.96 for nevus, and 0.97 for benign keratosis.

The integration of game theory for confidence evaluation and hierarchical decision-making enhances the transparency and explainability of the CAD system, addressing the 'black box' nature of traditional CNNs.

The system is effective for assisting dermatologists, particularly in clinical settings with atypical or difficult-to-diagnose pigmented lesions, and has potential as a didactic tool for less experienced dermatologists.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the handling of class imbalance and the lack of large-scale clinical validation in real-world scenarios, suggesting these as directions for future work.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; it was evaluated solely on the public ISIC 2018 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper acknowledges a limitation regarding class imbalance, noting that the approach only slightly addressed this issue and that future work is needed to mitigate it.

There is a lack of large-scale clinical validation; the usability and adoptability in real clinical scenarios were not sufficiently investigated, with only a small use-case analysis by one dermatologist.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed framework over prior works; comparisons relied on descriptive statistics.
The dataset used (ISIC 2018 subset) is imbalanced, with Nevi (6,705 lesions) significantly over-represented compared to Melanoma (1,113) and Benign Keratosis (1,099).

The system has not yet been deployed in clinical practice due to concerns about false negatives and the 'black box' nature of CNNs, which this study attempts to address through explainability features.
The code and models are made available online, promoting reproducibility, but the study does not report on data leakage risks or cross-validation strategies beyond five experimental runs.
